coredata.dc:title: Rapid microwave synthesis of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots as a novel turn off-on sensor for label-free determination of copper and etidronate disodium
coredata.dc:creator: El-Malla, Samah F.
Elshenawy, Eman A.
Hammad, Sherin F.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: An environmentally-friendly, selective and rapid turn off-on fluorescent probe for both copper (Cu2+) and etidronate disodium (ETD) detection was derived from nitrogen/sulfur co-doping of carbon dot (N,S-CD). The N,S-CDs were produced, for first time, through microwave-assisted pyrolysis in only 60 s using readily accessible and cheap precursors (citric acid and thiosemicarbazide). The proposed synthesis method resulted in N,S-CDs with narrow particle size distribution, high product yield (64.38 wt%) and high doping efficiency (N, 28.56% and S, 8.47%). The turn off-on sensor was developed after demonstrating the ability of Cu2+ to quench fluorescence of the produced CDs and probable fluorescence restoration through complexation of Cu2+ by the bisphosphonate group of ETD. The developed sensor showed a good performance for Cu2+ and ETD detection with a linear range of 5–125 μM and 30–210 μM, respectively. This developed platform could be applied for routine analysis of Cu2+ and ETD with good recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper
Etidronate
N,S doped carbon dots
Microwave synthesis
Turn off-on sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for Cu2+ and ETD assay
Optimization of Cu2+ and ETD sensing conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the N,S-CDs (A), HRTEM of the N,S-CDs; inset: lattice spacing of 0.20 nm (B), SAED pattern of the N,S-CDs (C) and TEM image of the N,S-CDs after Cu2+ addition (D).
IR spectrum presenting the surface functionality of the prepared CDs.
UV, excitation and emission spectra of the prepared CDs (A), and Emission spectra of the prepared CDs produced with excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 390 nm (B).
Stern–Volmer plots for quenching of the prepared CDs fluorescence by Cu2+.
UV absorption spectra of the prepared CDs with escalating Cu2+concentrations showing overlap with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the N,S-CDs.
Effect of ETD addition on the UV absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system.

Emission spectra of CDs with varying Cu2+ concentrations (A), and linear relationship between fluorescence quenching (F 0–F) and [Cu2+]; where fluorescence intensities: F 0 for N,S-CDs and F for N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system (B).
Selectivity of the developed sensor towards Cu2+ and ETD detection; response towards various metal ions (A), and response towards possible tablet interferents (B).

Fluorescence restoration on adding different ETD concentrations (A), and the linearity plot of fluorescence recovery against ETD concentration, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CDs-Cu2+ in absence and presence of ETD, respectively (B).
Synthesis of N,S-CDs and strategy for Cu2+ and ETD detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Sa, Sb, and Sy/x: Standard deviation of intercept, slope, and residuals, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the developed sensor for determination of ETD in commercial tablets.

head.ce:title: Rapid microwave synthesis of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots as a novel turn off-on sensor for label-free determination of copper and etidronate disodium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Services Center, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Services Center, Faculty of Pharmacy-Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Pharmacy, Elgeish Street, The Medical Campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Medical Campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An environmentally-friendly, selective and rapid turn off-on fluorescent probe for both copper (Cu2+) and etidronate disodium (ETD) detection was derived from nitrogen/sulfur co-doping of carbon dot (N,S-CD). The N,S-CDs were produced, for first time, through microwave-assisted pyrolysis in only 60 s using readily accessible and cheap precursors (citric acid and thiosemicarbazide). The proposed synthesis method resulted in N,S-CDs with narrow particle size distribution, high product yield (64.38 wt%) and high doping efficiency (N, 28.56% and S, 8.47%). The turn off-on sensor was developed after demonstrating the ability of Cu2+ to quench fluorescence of the produced CDs and probable fluorescence restoration through complexation of Cu2+ by the bisphosphonate group of ETD. The developed sensor showed a good performance for Cu2+ and ETD detection with a linear range of 5–125 μM and 30–210 μM, respectively. This developed platform could be applied for routine analysis of Cu2+ and ETD with good recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (II) is an essential trace element in the human body, being engaged in a variety of important metabolic activities. Copper (II) deficiency can cause several diseases including anemia, myeloneuropathy, and brittle bones [1,2]. On the other hand, excessive Cu2+ ion buildup in the body is very hazardous causing liver damage, as well as Wilson's and Alzheimer diseases, thus its determination is essential [3]. Accordingly, Environmental Protection Agency has listed Cu2+ as a significant pollutant and set its limit in drinking water to 20 μM [4]. Copper (II) can be determined by inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometry [5,6], atomic absorption [7,8] and atomic fluorescence spectroscopy [9]. However, these conventional methods typically involve difficult operations and need expensive equipment along with high consumption of hazardous organic solvents, limiting their use. Because of its simplicity, high sensitivity, and low cost, carbon dot (CD)-based sensors have gained a lot of interest for metal ions detection including Cu2+ detection [10–13].

Etidronate disodium (ETD) is a non-nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that is currently used to treat a variety of diseases including Paget disease of the bone, osteoporosis, multiple myeloma, malignancy-mediated hypercalcemia, and bone metastasis [14,15]. Being a non-chromophoric drug, the determination of ETD is challenging. A variety of methods for detecting ETD have been described so far, including ion pair chromatography [16], ion exchange chromatography [17–19], reversed phase liquid chromatography [20,21], liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry [22] and electrochemical methods [23,24]. These chromatographic methods require either unconventional detection modalities such as evaporative light-scattering detection or pre/post column derivatization processes and these sophisticated costly instrumentation, and time-consuming procedures limit their use for routine analysis. Furthermore, the few spectrophotometric methods [25,26], reported for ETD detection, suffer from long reaction time, corrosive solvents, or tedious derivatization steps. Consequently, developing a fast, affordable, simple, and green technique for ETD detection is essential.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of carbon nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm [27]. They have recently gained a lot of attention in many applications such as catalysis, sensors, bioimaging, and drug delivery owing to their interesting characteristics of high aqueous solubility, chemical and optical stability, feasibility of surface modification and strong biocompatibility [28,29]. The doping of CDs by introducing heteroatoms like N, S, P and B can influence their electronic/optical properties and surface reactivity [30–32]. Since N has an atomic radius close to that of C (radius of N: 0.075 nm, C: 0.077 nm), and S has an electronegativity similar to that of C (electronegativity of S: 2.58, C: 2.55), as well as the fact that S and N display various binding configurations, they are the most widely investigated [31,33]. Laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, chemical oxidation, and hydro/solvothermal treatment have all been proposed as ways to produce CDs [34]. However, these techniques have several disadvantages, including time-consuming approach, expensive starting materials, hazardous organic compounds, and the need for drastic conditions [35]. Microwave-assisted technique, on the other hand, offers many advantages like being simple, eco-friendly, and economical. Additionally, the microwave-heating characteristics confer the technique several advantages such as short reaction time, reduced energy consumption, improved reaction yields, and enhanced particle size uniformity [33,35].

In the current work, we describe for the first time, a green single-step microwave-assisted process for producing N,S-doped CDs utilizing citric acid (CA) and thiosemicarbazide (TSC). There are some reports [32,36,37] that describe the synthesis of N,S-CDs from these starting materials using hydrothermal treatment. However, in these reports a long reaction time, varying from 5 h to overnight, and external high temperature were required which in turn increase energy consumption and limit their applicability. Compared to these reports, our proposed synthesis method is rapid, needing only 60 s, energy-efficient with no external temperature requirement, and produce a high product yield. The fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N,S-CDs could be rapidly and selectively quenched by Cu2+, therefore the N,S-CDs were successfully implemented as a fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ detection in environmental water samples. Moreover, taking the advantage of the strong affinity of bisphosphonate groups towards Cu2+, a well-thought-out, fast, and cost-effective switch off-on probe applying the prepared N,S-CDs was developed for determining ETD without the need of any additional chemical reagent. In this sensing platform, fluorescence of CDs is quenched by Cu2+‏, afterwards the fluorescence could be restored by complexation of Cu2+‏ with ETD in a concentration dependent manner. As far as we know, no CDs-based fluorescence method has been described for ETD analysis.

The Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-6300, JASCO, Japan). UV absorption spectra were obtained using a double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were performed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV accelerating voltage. Fourier transform-infrared spectrum (FTIR) was recorded to identify the surface functional groups of the N,S-CDs (FTIR-4100, Jasco, Japan). A domestic microwave oven (1000 W), ultrasonic cleaner (3510, Branson, USA) and a benchtop centrifuge (Hettich, Germany) were used during the N,S-CDs synthesis. A calibrated pH-meter (pH211, HANNA, USA) was used for pH adjustment of buffer solutions.

CA and TSC were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Kandel, Germany). Copper sulfate pentahydrate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, magnesium sulfate, cobalt acetate, nickel sulfate hexahydrate, ferric chloride, barium chloride, chromium (III) sulfate hexahydrate, silver nitrate and lead acetate (laboratory grade) were purchased from ISO-Chem (Pithiviers, France). Lactose monohydrate, maltose, dextrin, hydroxy propyl methylcellulose (HPMC), tartaric acid, mannitol, sodium acetate, ammonium acetate, boric acid and sodium hydroxide were obtained from ADWIC company (Egypt). Conc HCl and glacial acetic acid (analytical grade) were obtained from AdventChembio (Mumbai, India). Quinine Sulfate was obtained from Alpha Chemika (Mumbai, India). Etidronate disodium (purity, 99.5%) was presented by Sigma Pharmaceutical industries (El Menofia, Egypt). Etronel® 200 mg tablets were purchased from local pharmacy. Water samples were obtained from Bahr Shebeen River Canal (El Menofia, Egypt).

Synthesis was achieved using microwave-assisted approach as follow: CA (0.192 g, 1 mmol) and TSC (0.274 g, 3 mmol) were pyrolyzed using domestic microwave (60 s) till attaining dark orange product. After allowing the product to cool to ambient temperature, it was dissolved in 20 mL distilled water by the aid of ultrasonication for 15 min. To remove the insoluble debris, the obtained orange solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 6000 rpm before being filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. The N,S-CDs solution was refrigerated at 4 °C for storage.

The production yield (PY) of the synthesized N,S-CDs was calculated according to Zheng et al. [38]. The N,S-CDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain the N,S-CDs solid, which was then weighed and the PY value was calculated according to the following equation:
PY =
(
m
N , S − CDs
/
m s
)
× 100
where mN,S-CDs refers to the mass of the N,S-CDs solid, and ms refers to the mass of the starting materials (CA and TSC).

The quantum yield (Q) was calculated employing the following equation:
Q x
=
Q
s t
(
I x
/
I
s t
)
(
A
s t
/
A x
)
(
η x 2
/
η
s t
2
)
where I is the integrated fluorescence after excitation at 350 nm, A represents the absorbance value at 350 nm, and
η
means the solvent's refractive index (
η
equals 1.33 for both solvents) [39]. The symbol “st” refers to quinine sulfate while “x” refers to N,S-CDs. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a reference fluorophore with Qst equals 0.54. The absorbance was kept below 0.1 in order to reduce the absorption effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for Cu2+ and ETD assay
ce:section.ce:para: In Cu2+ determination, typically 20 μL N,S-CDs (15 mg mL−1), 1 mL 0.05 M acetate buffer pH 3, and different volumes of Cu2+ stock solution (1.5 mM) were mixed, completed to 3 mL with distilled water and the fluorescence of the mixture was measured. Instead of Cu2+, the same procedure was carried out with different metal ions to investigate selectivity.

In a typical assay for ETD, 20 μL N,S-CDs (15 mg mL−1) was mixed with different volumes of ETD stock solution (0.9 mM), 1 mL 0.05 M acetate buffer pH 3, and 200 μL of Cu2+ solution (1.5 mM). The mixture was completed to 3 mL with distilled water, properly mixed and the fluorescence of the mixture was measured. The same procedure was repeated to test the selectivity for potential interfering materials instead of ETD. All fluorescence measurements were acquired at 430 nm following excitation at 350 nm, setting both excitation and emission band widths at 5 nm and the scanning speed at 1000 nm/min.

For detection of Cu2+, drinking and river water samples were collected and filtered through 0.45 μm membrane filter. The desired concentration of Cu2+ was added and 200 μL from each sample was analyzed according to the general procedure for Cu2+ determination.

For determination of ETD, ten (Etronel)® tablets were grinded and a quantity equivalent to 90 mg ETD was weighed, 50 mL distilled water was added, sonication for 10 min, then made up to 100 mL with distilled water. This solution was filtered using 0.45 μm filter and 100 μL of this solution was analyzed by the general procedure for ETD determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of Cu2+ and ETD sensing conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
The experimental conditions that could affect the sensitivity of the developed sensor, including pH, buffer volume, N,S-CDs concentration, Cu2+ concentration, and incubation time were studied and optimized. The effect of pH was examined on both Cu2+ and ETD detection across a pH range of 2.5–10. It was found that pH 3 resulted in the highest fluorescence intensity ratio (F
0 /F, where F
0 is the N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity and F is the N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system fluorescence intensity) for Cu2+ determination (Fig. S4). This can be explained by Cu2+ complexation with carboxylic acid, nitrile and thiocarbonyl groups found on N,S-CDs surface [44–47]. Similarly, pH 3 was the optimum pH for ETD sensing as it developed the highest fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F
0 where F
0 is the N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system fluorescence intensity and F is the N,S-CDs-Cu2+-ETD system fluorescence intensity) (Fig. S5). It is reported that a stable complex between Cu2+ and bisphosphonate groups is formed in acidic pH [48], thus ETD and N,S-CDs compete for Cu2+ complexation, resulting in partial recovery of the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. Fig. S6 shows the effect of buffer volume from 0.25 to 1.5 mL on the signal ratio for Cu2+ and ETD sensing and demonstrates that 1 mL buffer was the optimum. For both Cu2+ and ETD determination, the influence of CDs concentration across its linearity was studied on the fluorescence intensity ratio. From Fig. S7, N,S-CDs concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1 was the optimum concentration for both. The Cu2+ concentration influence on fluorescence recovery by ETD was examined (Fig. S8) and 100 μM Cu2+ was selected as the optimum concentration. For 20 min at 1 min intervals, the effect of incubation time on N,S-CDs-Cu2+ interaction and fluorescence recovery by ETD was investigated (Fig. S9). The developed sensor for Cu2+ and ETD determination has a fast and stable response which remained constant for 30 min.

Upon the addition of Cu2+, N,S-CDs fluorescence was considerably reduced. Stern−Volmer equation was employed to elaborate the potential quenching mechanism:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]

Considering fluorescence F
0 for N,S-CDs and F for N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system, K
SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant while Cu2+concentration is [Q] [49]. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F
0
/F) was examined at three different temperatures. Fig. 4 illustrated that the K
SV decreased with increasing temperature indicating the static quenching mechanism that involve a non-fluorescent complex in the ground state (K
SV equals 7.62 × 109 L mol−1, 6.74 × 109 L mol−1 and 5.88 × 109 L mol−1 at 298 K, 303 K and 313 K, respectively). The complex formation was further confirmed by examining the changes in the UV spectra of N,S-CDs when Cu2+ is added. It can be deduced from Fig. 5 that a new absorption peak appeared at 360 nm when Cu2+ was added, which increases with increasing Cu2+ concentration, proving the formation of the complex. The new peak of the formed complex with Cu2+ was also found to overlap with the excitation spectrum of N,S-CDs, which imparts the presence of a complementary inner filter effect mechanism [50] (Fig. 5). The effect of Cu2+ addition to the N,S-CDs appeared in TEM images that showed aggregation of the N,S-CDs when Cu2+ is present (Fig. 1D).

We also examined the mechanism of the developed sensor fluorescence recovery by ETD. Based on the strong affinity between bisphosphonate groups and Cu2+, when ETD is introduced to the N,S-CDs-Cu2+ system, it can complex Cu2+ reducing its impact on N,S-CDs fluorescence, allowing the fluorescence to be switched on (Fig. 6 ). This effect is proven by the decrease in the UV absorption peak at 360 nm, corresponding to N,S-CDs-Cu2+ complex, when ETD is introduced.

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence quenching (F 0–F) of the synthesized CDs increased quantitatively with addition of Cu2+ in the range 5–125 μM (Fig. 7 ). The regression equation was y = 2.639x + 54.112 and the detection limit (DL = 3.3 σ/slope, where σ is the SD of the intercept of a linearity plot around the detection limit) was 1.326 μM which is clearly below the United States Environmental Protection Agency's maximum allowed level of Cu2+ in drinking water (20 μM), Table 1 . To examine the selectivity of the sensor towards Cu2+ determination, various metal ions including Na+, Ba2+, Co2+, K+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Ag+, Pb2+ were tested (Fig. 8 A). These metal ions had a little influence on the N,S-CDs fluorescence, and only Cu2+ caused substantial fluorescence quenching indicating high selectivity of the developed probe towards Cu2+.

Because of the high specificity, sensitivity, and rapid reaction of the developed switchable sensor to Cu2+ and ETD, it was applied to detect Cu2+ in natural water samples and ETD in pharmaceutical formulation. Consequently, samples obtained from drinking and river water sources were tested for Cu2+ and these samples were analyzed following the procedure described in section 2.6. “Procedure for real sample analysis”. The results of the % recoveries, calculated from the relevant regression equation, and the % RSD are shown in Table 2 . As shown in the table, the % recoveries are within the accepted range and the % RSD values imply the method's precision.

Based on the rapid fluorescence recovery of N,S-CDs-Cu2+ by ETD, this established system was applied for ETD determination of in commercial tablets which are labeled to contain 200 mg ETD for each tablet. The samples were prepared and assayed following steps detailed under section 2.6. The % recoveries and the % RSD were estimated and statistically compared to those attained by the comparison method [25] which is based on oxidation of ETD with ceric (IV) sulfate in 0.5 M H2SO4 for 1 h (Table 3 ). The close agreement of the results indicates the reliability of the developed sensor for ETD determination. Unlike the comparison method, the developed sensor does not require long reaction time or the use of corrosive solvents.

For the first time, we successfully constructed a fluorescent turn off-on nano-probe based on a green, simple, rapid and inexpensive microwave synthesis of N,S-CDs utilizing CA and TSC (nitrogen/sulfur dopant) to serve as a novel probe for Cu2+ and ETD sensing. Various techniques were used to thoroughly explore the structural and optical characteristics of the CDs. Owing to the interaction of Cu2+ with surface groups on N,S-CDs, Cu2+ could considerably turn off fluorescence of N,S-CDs via both static quenching and inner filter effect mechanisms. Thus, this sensor was successfully employed for Cu2+ measurement in environmental water samples. In addition, the bisphosphonate group of ETD could compete with N,S-CDs for Cu2+ complexation, reducing its quenching impact on N,S-CDs and partially restoring the sensor's fluorescence. As a result, without the need for chemical derivatization, harsh reaction conditions, or a long reaction time that is required in previously reported methods, this simple and label-free sensing platform was employed as the first fluorescence-based determination of ETD allowing its use for routine analysis.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
All authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

Samah F. El-Malla: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Eman A. Elshenawy: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sherin F. Hammad: Resources, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fotouh R. Mansour: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
